hypothesis: Rounds 75 and 76 ruled out the simplest matched single-stage alternatives under the current `two_stage_residual` anchor: both `topk` and `jumprelu` were clearly worse at `K=128`, `EXPANSION_FACTOR=12`, `OPTIMIZER=signum`, and `LR=2.0e-3`. The next single matched architecture probe should therefore test a structurally different exportable decomposition rather than another plain selector. `codebook_topk` is the highest-value remaining yes/no probe because it still maps to a finite weighted sum over static vector libraries, but may reduce LUTurbo online coefficient and lookup cost by reusing a compact static basis instead of a full staged residual path. This round asks one question only: under the exact current mainline recipe, is `codebook_topk` competitive enough in full-curve behavior to remain worth later consideration, or should it be ruled out as clearly inferior to `two_stage_residual`?
summary: Use the one allowed matched architecture probe on `codebook_topk`. Keep the current mainline recipe fixed at `K=128`, `EXPANSION_FACTOR=12`, `OPTIMIZER=signum`, `LR=2.0e-3`, and the same aux/runtime settings, and change only `ARCHITECTURE` from `two_stage_residual` to `codebook_topk`. Judge the run by the whole trajectory, not just the endpoint: if `codebook_topk` stays reasonably close with a healthy curve, keep it in reserve because its shared-codebook structure may lower LUTurbo online lookup and coefficient cost; if it is clearly worse throughout, close the probe and return to the `two_stage_residual` mainline next round.
self_review: This obeys the Round 77 rule by changing exactly one primary variable, `architecture`, while keeping `K`, `EXPANSION_FACTOR`, `OPTIMIZER`, `LR`, and the rest of the recipe fixed. `codebook_topk` is a better use of the single probe than revisiting already-rejected plain selector variants because it tests a different cost-quality tradeoff that is still compatible with LUTurbo's static export contract. The main risk is that prior matched probing in an earlier regime already suggested weakness, but this remains the most informative remaining architecture-worthiness check under the current mainline anchor.
notes_to_memory: Round 77 spends the single allowed matched architecture probe on `codebook_topk` against the current `two_stage_residual` mainline recipe, because it represents a distinct static-library decomposition that may reduce LUTurbo online lookup and coefficient cost while preserving exportability.

Judge this probe as a family-worthiness test only: compare matched `codebook_topk@128` against the `two_stage_residual` reference at fixed `EF=12`, `signum`, and `LR=2.0e-3`, then return to the `two_stage_residual` mainline next round regardless of outcome.
next_hypotheses: If matched `codebook_topk` is reasonably close with a healthy curve, return to the `two_stage_residual` mainline next round but keep one later `codebook_topk` revisit available because its shared-codebook path may be cheaper to deploy.
If matched `codebook_topk` is clearly worse than `two_stage_residual` across the curve, close the probe and return to the `two_stage_residual` mainline next round.

If matched `codebook_topk` unexpectedly beats the current `two_stage_residual` reference, still return to the `two_stage_residual` mainline next round per policy, but record `codebook_topk` as a high-priority later follow-up family.
